problemStatement: How does Phase 4b's Solve Stage present a closing argument from current proof state?

EVID-1.statement: The proof's closure gate today checks ten conditions in a sequential block (six original structural conditions plus four added by cluster-A for Concerns and Resolve Conditions). Adding an eleventh condition fits the existing pattern.

EVID-2.statement: The closing argument today is agent-prose composed live in the final Solve Stage turn; it is not tracked in proof state, has no dedicated brief-template section, and is not derived from current state on any rebuild discipline.

EVID-3.statement: Withdrawn elements stay in the proof's elements map with status='withdrawn'; their statements, identifiers, and round-of-withdrawal are recoverable. Phantom-NC handling has its raw material on disk already.

EVID-4.statement: The proof MCP today returns graded signals (grounding coverage ratio, condition counts, ratified-vs-unratified RC counts, integrity warning counts, per-Concern coverage when locked) and boolean signals (closure_permitted with unmet reasons, stall detection over the last three rounds). It does not return graded readiness scores per RULE-22 property.

EVID-5.statement: Industry pattern (Event-B / Rodin): a persistent live ledger of proof obligations is the argument; no authored narrative. Pitfall: the 'reviewed' escape hatch lets users mark undischarged obligations as reviewed, producing a green ledger that hides un-proved claims.

EVID-6.statement: Industry pattern (NASA Verification Matrix close-out): tabular per-requirement coverage tracker. Closure means every row has a recorded result and signed closure artifact. Pitfall: tracks coverage, not argument quality.

EVID-7.statement: Industry pattern (Assurance 2.0 / Rushby): explicit defeater nodes in an argument graph; argument stays open as long as any defeater is unrefuted or unaccepted-as-residual-risk. The s(CASP) solver computes indefeasibility from current state. Closest industry precedent for living-derived arguments and explicit handling of withdrawn or contested claims.

EVID-8.statement: Cluster-B umbrella ran a 21-proof transition audit establishing the empirical baseline pattern (heavy-Round-1, NCs emerge through Solve, gated-exit). No analogous closure-baseline audit has been run; B.2 must commission one as foundational empirical work.

EVID-9.statement: Sprint 20260425-01 established that agent self-scoring is gameable from inside the agent — the agent can climb scores by writing better justifications without deepening understanding. Any closure-side scoring used to ground the request to move to closing argument cannot rely on agent self-scoring.

EVID-10.statement: Cluster-A locked the Resolve Condition shape (three fields: statement, problem_anchor, ratification), the Concern enumeration (PM-ratified, prefix CN-), sequential ratification, stale-ratification on revise, and closure conditions 7 through 10. These are inheritance-immutable for B.2 and are not relitigated.

RULE-1.statement: M-RULE-1: Five existing proof element types (Necessary Condition, Evidence, Rule, Permission, Risk) are immutable. Schemas, source rules, required fields cannot change.

RULE-2.statement: M-RULE-2: Phase naming is immutable — Phase 4a = Understand stage, Phase 4b = Solve stage.

RULE-3.statement: M-RULE-3: design-specify generates solutions; the proof produces input, specify owns architectural shape choice.

RULE-4.statement: M-RULE-4: Solve-drive is a feature of the agent; the design gives the drive a design-altitude landing target rather than prohibiting drive expression.

RULE-5.statement: M-RULE-5: Design = the problem (what), not the implementation (how). Phase 4a output stays at design altitude; mechanism vocabulary belongs in Phase 4b or downstream.

RULE-6.statement: M-RULE-7: Resolution-claim element carries five attributes — observable, designer-ratified, problem-statement-anchored, forward-looking, non-restrictive.

RULE-7.statement: B-RULE-11-19 (responsibility model bundle): designer authors Rules and Permissions; PM ratifies Concerns and Resolve Conditions; agent proposes, translates, and derives. Carries forward without re-derivation.

RULE-8.statement: B-RULE-20: Concerns iterate through proof — they are not locked once at opening; iteration discipline carries through Solve until closure.

RULE-9.statement: B-RULE-20.1: Agent may propose Concern changes or propose new Concerns at any time, subject to designer approval.

RULE-10.statement: B-RULE-21: Cluster-A planning stands as historical record; cluster-A vocabulary immutable; cluster-A implementation specifics open per PERM-1.

RULE-11.statement: S-RULE-1: Presentation gating — the closing argument is presented when the agent requests it and the designer approves the request. No automatic presentation, no presentation without approval.

RULE-12.statement: S-RULE-2: Request grounding — the agent's request to move to closing argument is grounded in objective measures, not agent open-ended judgment.

RULE-13.statement: S-RULE-3: Objective measures discipline — objective measures derive from proof state and proof MCP signals (grounding coverage, ratification counts, integrity warnings, per-Concern coverage, and equivalents); no separate agent self-scoring track.

RULE-14.statement: S-RULE-4 (revised under S-RULE-10): RC walk coverage — every ratified Resolve Condition is visited in the closing argument's walk; orphan RCs (ratified but un-visited) refuse closure. Under RC-keyed walk structure, each RC is the spine of a visit; constraining NCs appear inside each visit, not as the walk's backbone.

RULE-15.statement: S-RULE-5 (revised under S-RULE-10): Tension-naming requirement — at each Resolve Condition visit, the closing argument names the specific tension each constraining Necessary Condition imposes on that RC's resolution path; generic boilerplate fails the requirement. Tension prose lives per RC visit, indexed by the constraining NCs.

RULE-16.statement: S-RULE-6 (revised under S-RULE-10): Phantom-NC explicit handling — Necessary Conditions withdrawn during Solve are walked in a designated section of the closing argument (after the RC spine) with disposition tags: collapsed-into, superseded-by, or no-longer-relevant. Phantom NCs are not interleaved into the RC visits; they live in their own sub-walk so the structural primacy of the RC set as solution is not diluted.

RULE-17.statement: S-RULE-7: Living-document discipline — the closing argument is derived from current proof state; rebuilds on each approved presentation request, not authored once and frozen.

RULE-18.statement: S-RULE-8: Singular-focus scope — B.2 is concerned with Phase 4b closing-argument materialization only. Cluster-A relitigation is out, Phase 4b initialization is B.1 territory, Phase 4a redesign is cluster-C territory.

RULE-19.statement: S-RULE-9: Problem-statement self-containment — the Goal sentence stands on its own; no embedded references to specific Rules.

NCON-1.statement: The closing argument is structured as a per-ratified-Resolve-Condition walk: each RC visit names the Concern it resolves and the specific tension each constraining Necessary Condition imposes on this RC's resolution path. Phantom NCs (withdrawn during Solve) are walked in a designated post-spine section with disposition tags. Phantom RCs (revised-cleared or withdrawn) are walked in a parallel post-spine section with disposition tags. A top-level summary maps the ratified RC set to the Concerns of the problem statement so a PM can enter through their Concern frame before descending into the RC walk.
NCON-1.collapse_test: Without the RC-spine, S-RULE-10's 'RC set IS the solution' framing has no visible structural backing — the closing argument would walk constraints (NCs) or anchors (Concerns) instead of solution claims (RCs), inverting what the design is claiming. Without the top-level Concern-to-RC summary, the PM must mentally trace each RC back to its Concern at every visit, defeating PM-readability over a long RC list. Without the two phantom sub-walks, S-RULE-6 and S-RULE-11 have nowhere to live and the living-document discipline (S-RULE-7) breaks because retired claims silently disappear from the argument.
NCON-1.reasoning_chain: IF S-RULE-10 holds (the RC set IS the solution) AND S-RULE-4 holds (every ratified RC is visited) AND S-RULE-5 holds (each RC visit names per-NC tension on this RC's resolution path) AND S-RULE-6 holds (phantom NCs walked in a designated post-spine section) AND S-RULE-7 holds (rebuilds from current state) AND S-RULE-11 holds (phantom RCs walked in a parallel post-spine section) AND the responsibility-model bundle requires PM-readability, THEN the composition is an RC-spine walk plus two phantom sub-walks plus a top-level RC-to-Concern summary. No other shape lets all six rules hold simultaneously while keeping a PM's Concern-frame entry point.
rejected_alternatives: NC-keyed walk (the prior spine before S-RULE-10) — inverts S-RULE-10 by walking constraints instead of solution claims; reads as 'these are our restrictions' rather than 'this is our solution and these are the restrictions on it'.

Concern-keyed walk — puts Concerns at the spine; defeats S-RULE-10's structural claim that the RC set IS the solution; the Concerns become a spine they cannot semantically support since Concerns are aspects of the problem, not aspects of the solution.
Linear narrative prose without per-RC structure — loses the mechanical coverage check S-RULE-4 requires; 'every ratified RC visited' becomes an unverifiable property in continuous prose.

Tabular NC × RC matrix with tension prose in cells — sparse cells (where an NC doesn't constrain a particular RC) defeat S-RULE-5's specificity requirement; empty cells read as absence rather than as the tension's actual shape.

Claims-and-defenses graph in the Assurance 2.0 / s(CASP) pattern — overweight infrastructure for B.2's scope; the closing argument is a presentation artifact, not a graph database query result; defeats S-RULE-7's idempotent rebuild discipline because graph traversals carry hidden state.

RULE-20.statement: S-RULE-10: The set of Resolve Conditions is the solution. Not the Necessary Conditions, not the closure_permitted gate, not the brief envelope — the ratified RC set IS the design's solution claim. NCs constrain the shape of the solution; the closure_permitted gate verifies structural integrity; the brief carries the envelope; but the solution itself lives in the RC set.

RULE-21.statement: S-RULE-11: Phantom-RC explicit handling — Resolve Conditions whose ratification was cleared by revision (per cluster-A's stale-ratification mechanic) or that were withdrawn during Solve are walked in a designated section of the closing argument with disposition tags: revised-and-re-ratified, revised-and-pending, withdrawn-as-superseded, or withdrawn-as-no-longer-relevant. The closing argument cannot omit phantom RCs without violating the living-document discipline; under S-RULE-10 (RC set is the solution), accounting for retired solution claims is structurally required.

NCON-2.statement: The agent's request to move to closing argument fires when proof-state-derived objective measures clear three structural conditions simultaneously: (a) every signal in the composite reaches a per-signal floor; (b) the weighted aggregate of signals exceeds an aggregate threshold; (c) no integrity warnings are active. The composite signals draw from existing proof MCP machinery — per-Concern coverage, NC grounding coverage, RC ratification completeness, and equivalents — with thresholds designer-ratified before first use.
NCON-2.collapse_test: Without all three structural conditions, the trigger fails differently in each direction. Without per-signal floors, one strong signal masks weak signals — agent engineers entries against the strong signal and designer approves on a hidden gap. Without an aggregate threshold, all signals can sit at floor with no signal of overall readiness — trigger fires on minimal compliance. Without integrity-warning-zero, the trigger fires while the proof carries unresolved structural anomalies — designer approves a presentation built on stale grounding or ungrounded conditions. Cluster-B's understanding-stage transition gate uses the same triple-condition shape (per-tenet floor + overall threshold + no pending dispositions) — it's the precedent for resisting single-signal gameability.
NCON-2.reasoning_chain: IF S-RULE-1 holds (presentation only on agent request + designer approval) AND S-RULE-2 holds (request grounded in objective measures) AND S-RULE-3 holds (objective measures derive from proof state and proof MCP signals; no agent self-scoring) AND the 20260425-01 finding holds (single-signal scoring is gameable from inside the agent) AND the proof MCP returns multiple graded signals today (grounding coverage, condition counts, ratified RC counts, integrity warnings, per-Concern coverage), THEN the request trigger must clear three structural conditions at once — per-signal floor, weighted aggregate threshold, and integrity-warning-zero — because no single condition alone resists agent gameability while honoring all four upstream rules.
rejected_alternatives: Single-signal threshold on closure_permitted alone — closure_permitted is boolean and binary; doesn't surface graded readiness; trigger would only fire when closure was already permitted, defeating mid-development presentation requests.
Single-signal threshold on grounding coverage alone — gameable; agent climbs the ratio by adding ungrounded NCs that fall back on weak Evidence; one strong signal masks weak signals.
Agent open-ended judgment with no objective measure — directly defeated by S-RULE-2.
Designer-cued only (agent never requests; designer asks) — defeats S-RULE-1's 'agent requests' framing; reverses the human-in-the-loop direction.

Continuous numeric score the agent watches in real time — adds cognitive load without changing the binary fire-or-not decision; doesn't resist gaming since agent still optimizes against the visible number.
Time-based trigger (every N rounds, or after M state changes) — disconnected from proof readiness; defeats S-RULE-2's 'grounded in objective measures' — time is not a measure of proof state.

NCON-3.statement: The closing argument is derived from current proof state by a read-only idempotent computation that materializes the per-RC walk, the phantom-NC sub-walk, the phantom-RC sub-walk, and the top-level Concern-to-RC summary on each approved presentation request. The computation does not mutate proof state; the same input state always produces the same output argument; rebuilds happen on each approved request from current state, not incrementally from a prior presentation.
NCON-3.collapse_test: Without read-only discipline, derivation mutates state — a presentation request leaves residue, the next request rebuilds against mutated state plus residue; the closing argument drifts even when underlying proof hasn't changed; cascade fatigue (RISK-1) compounds because each rebuild differs without an underlying reason. Without idempotency, two rebuilds on the same state produce different arguments — designer ratifies one rendering, the next request shows a different rendering of the same proof, and the ratification's force evaporates because what was ratified isn't what's now visible. Without per-request rebuild from current state (incremental from prior instead), retired claims silently persist into the next argument and S-RULE-11 (phantom-RC handling) loses its mechanical guarantee.
NCON-3.reasoning_chain: IF S-RULE-7 holds (closing argument rebuilds from current state on each approved request, not authored once and frozen) AND NC-1 holds (RC-spine composition with two phantom sub-walks and a top-level summary) AND S-RULE-10 holds (the RC set IS the solution; the argument is showing the solution) AND the Assurance 2.0 / s(CASP) precedent shows living-derived arguments compute idempotently from state AND the proof MCP exposes get_proof_state as a read-only state loader today, THEN the derivation is a read-only idempotent computation that produces the same argument from the same state. Mutation would defeat 'rebuilds from current state' because rebuilds would be against state plus accumulated derivation residue; non-idempotent computation would defeat designer trust because two reads of the same proof would show differently.
rejected_alternatives: Mutating derivation (rebuild updates state with cached fragments) — defeats S-RULE-7's 'from current state'; introduces hidden derivation state that designer can't see.
Incremental derivation (rebuild diffs against last presentation) — accumulates derivation history alongside proof state; defeats S-RULE-7 because rebuilds aren't from current state alone.
Stored derivation snapshot (last argument cached, rebuild reads cache) — exact failure mode S-RULE-7 forbids ('authored once and frozen'); cache invalidation becomes a separate gameability surface.

Tool-call-only derivation (designer must invoke a separate tool to render) — defeats S-RULE-1's 'agent requests + designer approves' single approval cycle; splits one approval event into two operations.
